agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce/logistics system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and the relationship between the two.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase and receive goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment and beyond."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped. Intended to be available under certain pre-shipment conditions."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that locks in pricing for an order. Once created, Finance considers it irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Represents a key lifecycle boundary after which the PM agrees cancellation is no longer possible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Operations states that invoicing occurs at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it has shipped.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change the window (e.g., restrict to 30 minutes after placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request MUST succeed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats the invoice as a financial commitment with accounting and potentially legal implications. Reversing an invoice is not a simple policy change — it affects ledgers, tax reporting, and revenue recognition.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, a cancellation request MUST be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. Operations could choose to invoice at a later stage (e.g., at pick/pack or shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice MUST be created synchronously as part of the placement process."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed but has not yet shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The order is cancelled and its state transitions to 'Cancelled' (per PM requirement)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has been invoiced","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation is rejected with an error indicating invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (per Finance requirement)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order is being placed","when":"The placement process completes","then":"An invoice is automatically created and associated with the order (per Operations requirement)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"An order has already shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation is rejected because the shipment boundary has passed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed (and therefore immediately invoiced) — customer tries to cancel before shipment","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says allow it (not yet shipped), BR-2 says reject it (already invoiced). This is the core contradiction."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether cancellation applies to the unshipped portion; not addressed by any stakeholder."},{"scenario":"Invoice created but the invoicing system fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"If invoicing is asynchronous or fails, there may be a brief window where the order exists without an invoice — cancellation would be allowed under BR-2 but this contradicts BR-3's intent of immediate invoicing."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or refund issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but real-world accounting often uses credit memos. Whether a credit memo constitutes a de facto cancellation is unaddressed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked from placement onward, customer satisfaction drops and refund/chargeback costs may increase. If cancellation is allowed despite invoicing, Finance loses revenue recognition integrity and audit compliance.","operational":"The team cannot implement this feature without a resolution — the rules as stated produce a deadlocked state machine where no order can ever be cancelled (invoiced at placement, invoiced orders non-cancellable).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders shortly after placement. If the system forbids this due to immediate invoicing, it will generate support tickets and erode trust.","risk":"Implementing either interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks: (a) financial/audit violations if invoices are voided, or (b) customer churn and regulatory risk (consumer protection laws often mandate a cancellation window) if cancellation is impossible.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order if Finance+Ops rules win; or can cancel freely before shipment if PM rule wins — directly affects their experience."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Invoice integrity and revenue recognition are at stake. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders breaks their accounting model."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved later in the lifecycle to resolve the conflict, Operations loses the ability to lock in pricing at placement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of complaints if customers cannot cancel orders they just placed."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Many jurisdictions require a cooling-off period for online orders. A system that prevents all cancellations may violate consumer protection law."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), but orders should be cancellable before shipment (PM). Since every placed order is immediately invoiced, no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting the PM's requirement. The spec is internally inconsistent and cannot be implemented as stated.","high_confidence_areas":["The three rules as stated are mutually contradictory — this is provable by simple logic","The domain terms (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) are well-understood","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is likely a hard financial/accounting constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing (e.g., invoice at shipment) as a compromise","Whether Finance would accept a credit-memo-based reversal as distinct from invoice cancellation","Whether legal/regulatory cancellation windows override any of these business rules"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means the invoice exists before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit memo or reversal process that Finance considers acceptable","Shipment occurs strictly after placement (i.e., there is a nonzero time window between placement and shipment)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) and BR-2 (no cancellation after invoicing) combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) create a logical impossibility: every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, so no order can ever be cancelled, which directly negates the PM's cancellation requirement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A) Move invoicing to shipment time — preserves PM cancellation window but Operations loses price-lock-at-placement","B) Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' or 'pending invoice' state at placement that is reversible, with the final invoice issued at shipment — compromise that locks pricing without creating an irreversible financial document","C) Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via a credit memo process — preserves Operations' invoicing timing and PM's cancellation window, but Finance must accept that invoices can be effectively reversed","D) Define a short cancellation grace period (e.g., 30 minutes) during which the invoice is held in a provisional state — time-boxed compromise"],"recommendation":"Option B (pro-forma/provisional invoice at placement, final invoice at shipment). This preserves price-locking for Operations, maintains a cancellation window for the PM, and respects Finance's rule that finalized invoices are irreversible. It introduces a two-stage invoicing model that is standard in many logistics and wholesale businesses."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot be simultaneously satisfied. Implementing all three produces a system where order cancellation is advertised but functionally impossible.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. The cancellation feature would be dead code."}]}

Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and the relationship between the two.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase and receive goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- PM says cancellation is allowed before shipping
- Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled
- Operations says orders are invoiced at placement time
- Therefore: orders are invoiced immediately upon placement, meaning they can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced from the moment they exist), which directly contradicts the PM's requirement that they can be cancelled before shipping.


- This is a textbook contradiction, so I'm marking it with type "contradiction" and confidence at 50% or below.

Whether a credit memo constitutes a de facto cancellation is unaddressed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked from placement onward, customer satisfaction drops and refund/chargeback costs may increase. If cancellation is allowed despite invoicing, Finance loses revenue recognition integrity and audit compliance.","operational":"The team cannot implement this feature without a resolution — the rules as stated produce a deadlocked state machine where no order can ever be cancelled (invoiced at placement, invoiced orders non-cancellable).","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders shortly after placement. If the system forbids this due to immediate invoicing, it will generate support tickets and erode trust.","risk":"Implementing either interpretation without stakeholder alignment risks: (a) financial/audit violations if invoices are voided, or (b) customer churn and regulatory risk (consumer protection laws often mandate a cancellation window) if cancellation is impossible.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel any order if Finance+Ops rules win; or can cancel freely before shipment if PM rule wins — directly affects their experience."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Invoice integrity and revenue recognition are at stake. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders breaks their accounting model."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved later in the lifecycle to resolve the conflict, Operations loses the ability to lock in pricing at placement."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of complaints if customers cannot cancel orders they just placed."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Many jurisdictions require a cooling-off period for online orders. A system that prevents all cancellations may violate consumer protection law."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement (Ops), invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (Finance), but orders should be cancellable before shipment (PM). Since every placed order is immediately invoiced, no order can ever be cancelled, directly contradicting the PM's requirement. The spec is internally inconsistent and cannot be implemented as stated.","high_confidence_areas":["The three rules as stated are mutually contradictory — this is provable by simple logic","The domain terms (order, invoice, shipment, cancellation) are well-understood","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is likely a hard financial/accounting constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Operations would accept deferred invoicing (e.g., invoice at shipment) as a compromise","Whether Finance would accept a credit-memo-based reversal as distinct from invoice cancellation","Whether legal/regulatory cancellation windows override any of these business rules"],"assumptions":["Invoicing at placement means the invoice exists before any cancellation window opens","There is no existing credit memo or reversal process that Finance considers acceptable","Shipment occurs strictly after placement (i.e., there is a nonzero time window between placement and shipment)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) and BR-2 (no cancellation after invoicing) combined with BR-3 (invoice at placement) create a logical impossibility: every order is invoiced the instant it is placed, so no order can ever be cancelled, which directly negates the PM's cancellation requirement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A) Move invoicing to shipment time — preserves PM cancellation window but Operations loses price-lock-at-placement","B) Introduce a 'pro-forma invoice' or 'pending invoice' state at placement that is reversible, with the final invoice issued at shipment — compromise that locks pricing without creating an irreversible financial document","C) Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via a credit memo process — preserves Operations' invoicing timing and PM's cancellation window, but Finance must accept that invoices can be effectively reversed","D) Define a short cancellation grace period (e.g., 30 minutes) during which the invoice is held in a provisional state — time-boxed compromise"],"recommendation":"Option B (pro-forma/provisional invoice at placement, final invoice at shipment). This preserves price-locking for Operations, maintains a cancellation window for the PM, and respects Finance's rule that finalized invoices are irreversible. It introduces a two-stage invoicing model that is standard in many logistics and wholesale businesses."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated cannot be simultaneously satisfied. Implementing all three produces a system where order cancellation is advertised but functionally impossible.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is negated by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. The cancellation feature would be dead code."}]}
